front: Work: the narrator of this novel uses the pretentious pronoun 'wagahai' and belongs to the teacher Mr. Sneaze
Work: Avalon Coldmoon obsessively grinds glass balls to simulate light passing through a frog's eye in this novel
Work: the pretentious artist Waverhouse, the student Avalon Coldmoon, and Opula Goldfield appear in this novel
Work: Singleman Kidd encourages calmness as children keep intruding to retrieve baseballs in this novel
Work: K commits suicide leaving a note asking 'Why did I wait so long to die?' and Sensei later does the same after Emperor Meiji's death
Work: Sensei stole his landlady's daughter Ojosan from his friend K in this novel
Work: the second half of this novel is an autobiographical letter/testament from Sensei to the unnamed narrator
Work: Sensei says 'loneliness is the price we have to pay for being born in this modern age' and advises the narrator on his father's kidney disease
Work: Sensei insists 'there is guilt in love' and asks the narrator to consider what it means 'to be tied down by long black hair'
Work: K is a priest's son obsessed with 'the concentration of the mind' in this novel
Work: the narrator plays chess with his ill father atop a foot warmer in this novel
Work: colleagues have derisive nicknames: Red Shirt, Porcupine, the Clown, Hanger-on, and Squash
Work: the title math teacher and Porcupine beat up Red Shirt and the Clown for visiting a brothel
Work: children fill the protagonist's bed with grasshoppers, leaving his face swollen by mosquito bites
Work: the obsequious servant Kiyo gives the title character his nickname in this novel
Work: Red Shirt schemes to tarnish the reputation of a colleague nicknamed for resembling a squash, to steal an attractive middle school teacher
Work: an artist retreats to the mountains and meets the mysterious O-Nami, who reminds him of Millais's Ophelia
work: Kusamakura / The Three-Cornered World / Grass Pillow (1906)

front: Work: the opening sentence of this novel praises the turbidity of one's life; described as its author's most lyrical novel
work: Kusamakura / The Three-Cornered World / Grass Pillow (1906)

front: Work: the twelfth chapter examines Misao Fujimura's suicide at Kegon Falls and his death poem
work: Kusamakura / The Three-Cornered World / Grass Pillow (1906)

front: Work: a character considers the vanity of an artist delaying suicide by a week to finish a painting
work: Kusamakura / The Three-Cornered World / Grass Pillow (1906)

front: Work: the title student is fascinated by Thomas Browne's Hydriotaphia, Urn Burial
Work: this novel fictionalized Lafcadio Hearn's departure from the University of Tokyo
Author: this Japanese author succeeded Lafcadio Hearn as professor of English literature at the University of Tokyo
Author: this author's reminiscences on an unpleasant stay in the U.K. are quoted in Donald Keene's 'The Japanese Idea of Beauty'
Author: this author is thematically paired with Machado de Assis in Edwin Frank's Stranger Than Fiction
Author: in Murakami's Kafka on the Shore, Kafka reads The Miner by this author
Work: in one story from this collection, a man waits a century by a grave and sees a white lily blossom
Author: a businessman ostracized for his love marriage fails to understand a koan about his face before birth in this author's novel The Gate
